cards.front: For the high-yield GI parasites, what single buzzword detail (a stool finding, a deficiency, or a complication) lets you name each on sight?
cards.back: Each major GI parasite has a signature tell: Entamoeba gives bloody dysentery with flask-shaped ulcers and an anchovy-paste liver abscess; Cryptosporidium gives acid-fast oocysts with watery diarrhea in HIV; Diphyllobothrium causes B12 deficiency; hookworm causes iron-deficiency anemia; pinworm causes perianal itch with a positive tape test. Pattern recognition of one detail identifies the organism.

cards.front: A patient with bloody dysentery has colonic flask-shaped ulcers and a right-lobe liver abscess with anchovy-paste contents. Which parasite is responsible and how is it treated?
cards.back: Entamoeba histolytica (amoebiasis). The classic triad is bloody dysentery, flask-shaped colonic ulcers, and an anchovy-paste liver abscess, treated with metronidazole.

cards.front: An HIV patient has watery, non-bloody diarrhea and stool shows acid-fast oocysts. Which two protozoa should you name?
cards.back: Acid-fast oocysts with watery diarrhea point to Cryptosporidium (classically in HIV) or Cyclospora. Both are acid-fast protozoa identified on modified acid-fast stain of stool.

cards.front: Match each parasite to its signature complication: Diphyllobothrium, Echinococcus, and Clonorchis. What is the key management caveat for the Echinococcus lesion?
cards.back: Diphyllobothrium (fish tapeworm) causes B12 deficiency; Echinococcus causes a hydatid cyst that must not be aspirated (risk of anaphylaxis and seeding); Clonorchis (liver fluke) predisposes to cholangiocarcinoma.

cards.front: Ingesting the eggs of Taenia solium (rather than larval cysts in pork) leads to which dangerous condition?
cards.back: Ingesting Taenia solium eggs causes cysticercosis, including neurocysticercosis when cysts lodge in the central nervous system. Eating undercooked pork with larval cysts instead causes intestinal taeniasis.

cards.front: Name the identifying tell for each intestinal nematode: whipworm, pinworm, hookworm, and Ascaris.
cards.back: Whipworm (Trichuris) causes rectal prolapse; pinworm (Enterobius) causes perianal itch with a positive tape test; hookworm causes iron-deficiency anemia; Ascaris causes Loffler syndrome (pulmonary phase) and, in heavy burden, bowel obstruction.

cards.front: Management of GI parasites collapses into a few drug families. Which drug covers most protozoa, which covers most worms, and which is the fluke drug?
cards.back: Metronidazole is the workhorse for tissue-invasive protozoa such as Entamoeba and Giardia; albendazole or mebendazole covers most nematodes (worms); and praziquantel is the drug for flukes (trematodes) and most tapeworms. Echinococcus is the exception, treated with albendazole rather than praziquantel.

cards.front: Which antiprotozoal covers Entamoeba and Giardia, and which different agent is used for Cyclospora and Cystoisospora?
cards.back: Metronidazole treats Entamoeba and Giardia. Cyclospora and Cystoisospora are treated with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) instead.

cards.front: What is the default drug for most intestinal nematodes, and which two nematodes need a different agent (and what is it)?
cards.back: Most nematodes are treated with albendazole or mebendazole. Pinworm can also be treated with pyrantel pamoate, and Strongyloides requires ivermectin.

cards.front: Praziquantel covers tapeworms, Schistosoma, and flukes, but which cestode is the exception and what is used instead?
cards.back: Echinococcus is the exception: it is treated with albendazole, not praziquantel. Praziquantel remains the drug of choice for other tapeworms, Schistosoma, and liver/intestinal flukes.

cards.front: The GI fungal section covers just two organisms. For each, what is the host setting and the identifying microscopic clue?
cards.back: Candida albicans is the commonest GI fungus, causing thrush and esophagitis in immunocompromised/HIV patients or after antibiotics, and shows pseudohyphae. Histoplasma is a dimorphic fungus acquired from bird/bat droppings that disseminates in the immunocompromised and appears as intracellular yeast within macrophages.

cards.front: What is the most common GI fungal infection, in which hosts does it cause esophagitis, and what is seen on microscopy?
cards.back: Candida albicans is the most common GI fungal infection. It causes oral thrush and esophagitis in immunocompromised or HIV patients and after antibiotic use, and shows pseudohyphae on microscopy.

cards.front: Which dimorphic fungus is acquired from bird and bat droppings, disseminates in the immunocompromised, and appears as intracellular yeast in macrophages?
cards.back: Histoplasma capsulatum. It is a dimorphic fungus acquired from bird and bat droppings, disseminates in immunocompromised hosts, and is identified as small intracellular yeasts within macrophages.

cards.front: The two GI fungi map to two antifungal strategies. What is first-line for Candida and for Histoplasma, and when do you escalate?
cards.back: For Candida, fluconazole is first-line (nystatin suffices for mild oral disease), escalating to an echinocandin or amphotericin B for severe or systemic infection. For Histoplasma, itraconazole covers mild-to-moderate disease, while severe or disseminated infection starts with amphotericin B and then steps down to itraconazole.

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for Candida esophagitis, when is nystatin sufficient, and how do you treat severe or systemic disease?
cards.back: Fluconazole is first-line for Candida esophagitis. Nystatin is adequate for mild oral thrush. Severe or systemic candidiasis is treated with an echinocandin or amphotericin B.

cards.front: How does treatment of Histoplasma differ between mild-moderate and severe/disseminated disease?
cards.back: Mild-to-moderate histoplasmosis is treated with itraconazole. Severe or disseminated disease is started on amphotericin B first, then stepped down to itraconazole.
